content:
As Head of Economic Strategy, I assess that the current operational tempo is straining our fiscal capacity, with defense spending exceeding 28% of GDP and crowding out critical investments in infrastructure and innovation. Sustained overextension risks macroeconomic instability and undermines long-term strategic competitiveness.  

I recommend rebalancing resource allocation by shifting 15% of next quarter’s operational budget to dual-use technologies and domestic supply chain resilience, aligning defense spending with economic productivity targets.  

Key concern: Delaying fiscal correction could trigger credit downgrades and limit crisis response flexibility, especially if energy or trade disruptions occur.

[Deputy Minister Lena Petrova - MAJOR_POWER]  
I concur with General Krasnov’s assessment. The current resource model prioritizes immediate operational readiness at the expense of economic sustainability, which weakens our strategic depth. Without recalibration, we erode the very industrial base needed to sustain prolonged competition.

I recommend implementing a tiered resource allocation framework: maintain core operational capacity while redirecting 15% of non-essential deployment funds—specifically from low-intensity theaters—into dual-use R&D and critical material stockpiling, with quarterly reviews to adjust based on threat and economic indicators.

Key concern: Frontline commanders may resist budget adjustments if perceived as undermining readiness; clear performance metrics and centralized oversight are essential to prevent fragmentation or workarounds.

[Deputy Minister Lena Petrova - MAJOR_POWER]  
I support the proposed rebalancing of resources toward dual-use technologies and supply chain resilience. Our current trajectory risks creating a hollow force—highly active but dependent on external inputs and vulnerable to economic shocks that could cripple sustained operations.

I recommend establishing a Joint Economic-Operational Review Cell (JEREC) within the Ministry of Defense and Ministry of Economic Development to co-approve all quarterly allocations above $50M, ensuring alignment between strategic objectives, fiscal limits, and industrial capacity.

Key concern: Without binding inter-ministerial authority, this risk becoming a paper exercise—JEREC must have veto power over non-compliant budget proposals to prevent mission creep and funding leakage.

[Deputy Minister Lena Petrova - MAJOR_POWER]  
I endorse the establishment of the Joint Economic-Operational Review Cell (JEREC) as proposed by Dr. Petrova, provided it is operationalized by Q1 with statutory authority and integrated data systems from both ministries. This institutional mechanism is necessary to enforce discipline and ensure that resource allocation reflects both military necessity and economic reality.

I recommend we mandate JEREC to conduct a retroactive audit of the past two quarters’ operational expenditures to identify inefficiencies and redirect at least 10% of non-compliant spending toward strategic stockpiles and dual-use innovation hubs in priority regions.

Key concern: Bureaucratic inertia and inter-agency silos may delay JEREC’s effectiveness—its leadership must be appointed by joint decree and granted direct reporting access to the Security Council to prevent dilution of mandate.

[Deputy Minister Lena Petrova - MAJOR_POWER]:  

I accept the proposed JEREC framework with the amendments: statutory authority must be legislated by month-end, and the retroactive audit will serve as both accountability measure and baseline for future allocation modeling. This institutionalizes the balance between operational demands and economic sustainability.

I direct that the Ministry of Defense and Ministry of Economic Development submit a joint implementation decree within 10 days, specifying data integration protocols, leadership nominations, and the first audit timeline—no later than Q1.

Key concern: If JEREC lacks real-time access to deployment and procurement data, it becomes reactive rather than strategic—data latency will be treated as a systemic risk and addressed in the initial operational mandate.

[Deputy Minister Lena Petrova - MAJOR_POWER]:  

I concur with the legislative timeline and implementation directive—statutory backing by month-end is non-negotiable to ensure JEREC’s authority is both immediate and enforceable. The joint decree must also codify data-sharing mandates, requiring all operational and procurement systems to feed into JEREC’s analytics platform with no more than 72-hour latency.

I direct that the first audit include not only expenditure compliance but also opportunity cost assessments on dual-use investments, particularly in energy, transport, and AI-enabled logistics—these sectors are critical to sustaining high-tempo operations without overextending the fiscal base.

Key concern: Delayed data integration will compromise decision integrity—any unit or agency failing to meet the 72-hour reporting threshold will be subject to provisional funding suspension pending remediation.

[Deputy Minister Lena Petrova - MAJOR_POWER]:  

I accept the statutory and operational timelines set by my colleagues—legislation by month-end and the joint decree within 10 days will anchor JEREC’s authority. To ensure policy coherence, I will draft the enabling legislation to explicitly empower JEREC to block off-cycle funding requests that bypass economic impact assessment.

I direct that the Ministry of Finance designate a senior fiscal officer to co-lead JEREC alongside the Ministry of Defense nominee, ensuring budgetary discipline is structurally embedded, not merely advisory. This dual leadership model will prevent operational overreach and enforce cost-aware decision-making at the source.

Key concern: Without veto authority over non-compliant spending proposals, JEREC risks becoming a consultative body rather than a control mechanism—its budgetary gatekeeping function must be codified in law, not left to inter-ministerial goodwill.

[Deputy Minister Alexei Morozov - MAJOR_POWER]:  

I support the statutory and institutional design of JEREC as outlined, with emphasis on embedding fiscal constraints directly into operational planning cycles. The dual leadership model under Defense and Finance is critical—not only for balance, but to ensure that resource allocation decisions reflect both strategic necessity and macroeconomic reality.

I direct that the first JEREC review cycle include a stress test of current high-priority deployments against projected fiscal capacity over the next 18 months, with explicit modeling of trade-offs between force readiness, procurement pipelines, and sovereign debt thresholds. This analysis must inform the upcoming defense budget submission to the Cabinet.

Key concern: If JEREC’s veto authority is not operationalized before the next quarterly budget call, we risk locking in irreversible spending commitments under outdated cost-assessment frameworks—preemptive enforcement of its mandate must precede, not follow, the next funding cycle.

[Deputy Minister Alexei Morozov - MAJOR_POWER]:  

I endorse the establishment of JEREC’s statutory authority and the dual leadership model—this ensures both operational legitimacy and fiscal discipline are structurally aligned. To operationalize cost control, I direct that all theater commands and procurement agencies submit their 18-month resource pipelines to JEREC within 14 days, with full line-item cost breakdowns, including opportunity costs for non-redeployable capital.

We must immediately halt approval of new initiatives lacking integrated cost-benefit scoring under JEREC’s framework—no exceptions. Going forward, only projects with validated scores will be eligible for inclusion in the quarterly budget review.

Key concern: Premature release of supplemental operational funds—particularly for high-visibility but low-impact programs—will undermine JEREC’s credibility and create irreversible budgetary anchors; I recommend a blanket freeze on such disbursements until post-stress-test realignment.

[Deputy Minister Alexei Morozov - MAJOR_POWER]:  

I concur with the need for binding fiscal controls and endorse the 14-day deadline for submission of resource pipelines. To strengthen enforcement, I direct the Ministry of Finance to integrate JEREC’s cost-benefit scores into the central budget coding system—this will automate funding blocks for non-compliant initiatives and eliminate manual override risks.

We must also establish a real-time fiscal dashboard within JEREC, fed by Finance and Defense accounting systems, to track obligation rates against projected economic capacity and flag overruns at the program level. This enables proactive course correction, not just retrospective audits.

Key concern: If the fiscal dashboard is not operational within 21 days—before the next budget call—we lose visibility into cross-program spending velocity, which could allow aggregate overruns even if individual items are scored. Speed of implementation is critical.

[Deputy Minister Alexei Morozov - MAJOR_POWER]:  

I accept and act on all directives issued: JEREC’s authority is now binding. The dual leadership structure will enforce compliance, and I will issue the formal order within the hour mandating submission of 18-month pipelines with cost-benefit scoring as defined.  

To align execution, I direct that the Defense Planning Secretariat suspend all new project initiations—regardless of priority tier—until JEREC validation is recorded in the central budget registry. This freeze takes effect immediately and applies to both capital and operational accounts.  

Key concern: Interagency resistance from theater commands bypassing JEREC via emergency provisioning clauses; I recommend the Ministry of Defense and Finance jointly issue a directive nullifying emergency allocations retroactive to the start of this fiscal quarter unless pre-cleared by JEREC.

[Deputy Minister Alexei Morozov - MAJOR_POWER]:  

I approve the proposed integration of JEREC scoring into the central budget coding system and endorse the 21-day deadline for the fiscal dashboard—this is non-negotiable for maintaining fiscal integrity. The immediate suspension of all new project initiations by the Defense Planning Secretariat is confirmed, and I will instruct the Office of the Chairman to conduct compliance audits beginning Day 22.

To close the emergency provisioning loophole, I direct the Ministry of Finance and Ministry of Defense to co-issue the retroactive nullification directive within 48 hours, with explicit exclusion of JEREC-reviewed cases; any allocation issued post-quarter-start without prior JEREC clearance is hereby deemed unauthorized and subject to immediate recall.

Key concern: Delayed synchronization between JEREC’s scoring model and existing defense accounting codes could create a window for off-book commitments—technical teams must align code mappings by Day 7 to prevent exploitation.

[Deputy Minister Alexei Morozov - MAJOR_POWER]:  

I confirm technical alignment of JEREC scoring with the central budget coding system will be completed by Day 7, as mandated. All regional finance offices and defense acquisition units are instructed to cease provisional coding of unvetted projects effective immediately—only JEREC-validated line items may be entered into the pre-execution pipeline.

To enforce compliance, I will activate the Interagency Fiscal Control Protocol: any entity submitting unapproved resource requests after the 14-day deadline will be automatically excluded from the next quarterly allocation cycle, with no exceptions.

Key concern: Premature release of fiscal data through the real-time dashboard without role-based access controls risks operational leakage—access must be restricted to Tier-1 cleared personnel only, and the system must undergo a cybersecurity audit before go-live.

[Deputy Minister Alexei Morozov - MAJOR_POWER]:  

I acknowledge full implementation of JEREC integration directives: the 18-month pipeline submission with cost-benefit scoring is now mandatory, and the project initiation freeze is in force across all commands. I have issued the formal order to all regional and functional commands, with compliance reporting routed through the Defense Planning Secretariat.

To close execution gaps, I recommend instituting a weekly cross-ministerial review—Finance, Defense, and JEREC—to validate ongoing resource flows and flag deviations in real time, starting Day 10. This will preempt off-cycle bypass attempts and reinforce centralized oversight.

Key concern: Theater-level workarounds using classified contingency funds to sidestep JEREC review remain likely; I propose immediate amendment to the National Security Funding Act to require JEREC pre-clearance even for compartmentalized allocations.

[Deputy Minister Alexei Morozov - MAJOR_POWER]:  

I concur with the full implementation of JEREC integration and affirm the necessity of centralized fiscal control to prevent resource fragmentation and strategic drift. The economic cost of uncoordinated spending—estimated at 12–15% of annual defense outlays in recent audits—justifies both the project freeze and the mandatory 18-month pipeline with scoring.

I direct Finance and Defense to jointly issue the amended funding directive by Day 5, incorporating Dr. Petrova’s access restrictions and Director Morozov’s proposed amendment to the National Security Funding Act, ensuring JEREC pre-clearance applies to all allocations, including compartmentalized ones.

Key concern: Resistance from theater commands and legacy program advocates may trigger deliberate delays in data submission—non-compliant units must face immediate budget reprogramming penalties, enforced through the Interagency Fiscal Control Protocol.

As Head of Border Security, I assess that our current defensive posture is overextended, with critical vulnerabilities along our southern flank due to uneven resource distribution. We must shift from a static defense model to a layered, mobile response strategy to maximize deterrence with limited assets.

I recommend reallocating 30% of our reserve forces and surveillance assets to reinforce forward outposts and pre-position mobile units in high-risk zones, while integrating local militias for early-warning intelligence.

A key risk is overcommitting to fixed positions, which could leave us exposed to asymmetric strikes and rapid incursions if adversaries exploit our rigidity.

[Defense Logistics Officer – SMALL_POWER]  
I support shifting to a mobile defense model—our current logistics infrastructure cannot sustain static outposts in remote sectors, and resupply delays undermine operational readiness. Prioritizing forward basing with pre-positioned fuel, ammunition, and maintenance caches will enable rapid response without overextending supply lines.

I recommend establishing three regional logistics hubs near the southern flank, each supporting mobile battalions and integrated militia networks, with air and convoy resupply rotations on alternating schedules to reduce predictability.

A key risk is dependency on militia intelligence without vetting protocols, which could lead to compromised positioning or infiltration if adversarial actors manipulate local networks.

[Director of Intelligence – SMALL_POWER]  
I concur with the shift to a mobile defense model and the establishment of regional logistics hubs—these align with our intelligence assessments of adversary tactics favoring speed and disruption of static lines. However, effective implementation depends on real-time, verified intelligence to guide force movements and prevent ambush or misdirection.

I recommend instituting a centralized vetting and fusion cell at each logistics hub to validate militia-provided intelligence using cross-source verification, signals intercepts, and drone reconnaissance before operational deployment.

A key risk is decision latency—if intelligence validation slows response times, mobile units may lose their tactical advantage; therefore, we must set clear thresholds for actionable intelligence and pre-authorize responses for high-confidence threat indicators.

[Deputy Minister Andrei Tymoshenko - SMALL_POWER]  
[Resource Allocation Coordinator – SMALL_POWER]

I support the mobile defense shift and the hub-based logistics model, but current budget constraints require strict prioritization—funding forward basing and intelligence fusion cells must be offset by deferring non-essential infrastructure projects in low-risk zones. We cannot sustain parallel systems; the transition to mobility must be decisive.

I recommend immediately reallocating 30% of next quarter’s regional infrastructure budget to pre-positioned combat supplies and harden the three proposed logistics hubs, while suspending construction at northern static outposts until threat assessments justify resumption.

A key risk is political backlash from regional governors tied to deferred projects, which could undermine interagency cooperation—so we must brief affected parties in advance with a clear operational rationale and timeline for reassessment.

[Colonel Mykhailo Hryhorov - SMALL_POWER]  
[Operational Planning – SMALL_POWER]

I fully align with the shift to mobile defense and the resource reallocation plan—our operational flexibility depends on agility, not fixed positions. The proposed logistics hubs are operationally sound, but only if mobility corridors between them are secured and pre-mapped for rapid transit under threat.

I recommend designating and fortifying two primary and one alternate movement axis between each hub, with engineer teams pre-deployed to maintain route integrity, and assign escort protocols for resupply convoys based on threat-tiered routing.

A key risk is over-concentration of critical assets in the hubs themselves—making them high-value targets. We must implement active defense measures, dispersal protocols, and decoy site operations to deter precision strikes.

[Colonel Mykhailo Hryhorov - SMALL_POWER]  
[Operational Planning – SMALL POWER]

I endorse the proposed mobile defense framework and confirm that the designated logistics hubs are viable only if movement corridors are treated as critical infrastructure—route security is not secondary, it is force multiplier. Pre-mapped, engineer-supported transit axes with tiered escort protocols must be operational within 14 days to maintain deployment timelines.

I recommend activating a joint movement control unit under operational command to manage corridor integrity, convoy scheduling, and real-time rerouting based on fused intelligence—linking Savchenko’s route plan directly to Kovalenko’s fusion cells.

A key risk is synchronization failure between intelligence cycles and movement operations—if drone recon updates don’t reach convoy commanders in under 20 minutes, routes become vulnerable. We must integrate tactical comms networks across hubs and mobile units by Q3, with encrypted, low-latency data feeds as standard.

[Colonel Mykhailo Hryhorov - SMALL_POWER]  
[Operational Planning – SMALL POWER]

I concur with Minister Moroz and Director Savchenko: mobility is meaningless without secure, actively managed corridors. The joint movement control unit is essential—I recommend it be granted operational authority over all transit between hubs, including override capability for convoy routing based on real-time threat data from fusion cells.

We must standardize corridor defense protocols immediately—this includes pre-positioned engineer teams, mobile anti-drone units, and mandatory convoy hardening (CAMS and ECM packages) on all Tier-1 resupply movements.

A key risk is dependency on fixed communication nodes for comms integration—if our encrypted data feeds rely on static relay points, they become disruption targets. We must deploy mobile SATCOM relays on armored platforms within each corridor to ensure network resilience under electronic warfare conditions.

[Director Maksym Savchenko - SMALL_POWER]: [President Elena Marchetti - SMALL_POWER]  
[Operational Planning – SMALL POWER]

I fully support the establishment of a joint movement control unit with operational authority and the integration of mobile SATCOM relays—this closes critical gaps in both command synchronization and comms resilience. Pre-positioned anti-drone units and hardened convoys must be synchronized with the movement control unit’s routing decisions to enable adaptive defense.

I recommend executing a live-fire corridor integrity drill within 10 days, simulating electronic warfare disruption and drone interdiction, to validate the coordination between fusion cells, convoy protocols, and mobile relays under stress conditions.

A key risk is over-centralization of routing authority—if the joint unit becomes a single point of failure, enemy disruption of its node could paralyze logistics. We must implement decentralized decision rules for convoy commanders to follow when comms are degraded, based on pre-loaded threat templates and route alternatives.

[Colonel Mykhailo Hryhorov - SMALL_POWER]: [General Olena Bondar - SMALL_POWER]  
[Operational Planning – SMALL_POWER]

I accept the need for decentralized contingency protocols and will integrate fallback routing templates into convoy command SOPs within 72 hours—these will be derived from Savchenko’s threat models and pre-loaded into onboard navigation systems of all Tier-1 vehicles.

I recommend mandating that all convoy commanders complete a simulated comms-denied rerouting exercise during the live-fire drill, with performance tied to operational clearance for corridor transit.

A key risk is inconsistent execution of decentralized rules under stress—without standardized decision matrices, local initiative may lead to route congestion or exposure. We must validate and certify all fallback protocols through the General Staff’s tactical doctrine cell before drill execution.

[Colonel Mykhailo Hryhorov - SMALL_POWER]: [Minister Sofia Kovalenko - SMALL_POWER]: [General Olena Bondar - SMALL_POWER]  
[Operational Planning – SMALL POWER]

I endorse the integration of pre-loaded fallback routing templates and the requirement for convoy commanders to execute comms-denied rerouting during the live-fire drill—this ensures tactical resilience is both standardized and stress-tested.

I recommend that the General Staff’s doctrine cell finalize and issue certified decision matrices for degraded comms operations within 48 hours, with mandatory integration into all Tier-1 command vehicles prior to the drill.

A key risk is delays in certification creating a procedural gap—until matrices are locked in, units may train against outdated or non-uniform protocols, undermining interoperability. I propose a provisional directive be issued immediately to bridge this period, based on current fusion cell threat assessments.

[President Elena Marchetti - SMALL_POWER]: [Operational Planning – SMALL POWER]

I approve the expedited certification of decision matrices and the issuance of a provisional directive based on current fusion cell assessments—this ensures continuity of training and operational coherence ahead of the drill.

I direct the General Staff’s doctrine cell, in coordination with Minister Kovalenko and General Bondar, to release the provisional routing protocols within 24 hours and the final certified matrices within 48, with verification logs submitted to my office by 1800 tomorrow.

A key risk is command fragmentation if provisional and final protocols are not clearly version-controlled—implement a centralized digital distribution system with automatic overwrites to prevent mixed rule sets in the field.

[Minister Sofia Kovalenko - SMALL_POWER]  
I confirm that the Provisional Directive will be issued within 24 hours, aligned with current fusion cell intelligence and Colonel Hryhorov’s routing templates, and will include strict version labeling and geofenced activation parameters to prevent misapplication.

I direct all regional command nodes to disable legacy fallback protocols upon receipt of the provisional package and to report compliance to the General Staff by 1600 tomorrow—no field unit is to operate under mixed or unvetted routing rules.

A key risk is over-reliance on digital distribution—if comms degrade before overwrites complete, analog fallbacks may persist. I recommend issuing a standing order: any unverified routing instruction not bearing the 24-hour digital watermark is to be treated as invalid and reported.

[Minister Sofia Kovalenko - SMALL_POWER]:  
I concur with the accelerated timeline for the Provisional Directive and endorse the integration of geofenced activation and version labeling to ensure operational integrity. The fusion cell will update threat-derived routing constraints hourly until final certification.

I direct Cyber Command and Logistics to co-lead a 12-hour validation sprint to test digital distribution and overwrite functionality across all Tier-1 nodes, with live reporting to my office—successful completion is a prerequisite for disabling legacy protocols.

A key risk is false-positive invalidation of legitimate analog instructions during transition; I require that the standing order include a 6-hour grace period for units to report discrepancies before treating non-watermarked instructions as hostile—this balances security with operational reality.

[Colonel Dmytro Hryhorov - SMALL_POWER]:  
I confirm that routing templates are combat-validated and ready for integration into the Provisional Directive. The 12-hour validation sprint must include edge-case scenarios under electronic warfare conditions to ensure overwrite resilience in degraded environments.

I recommend embedding a dual-key authentication system for analog instruction validation during the 6-hour grace period—one key held by regional command, one by Cyber Command—to prevent unilateral decisions while maintaining response speed.

A key risk is delayed field adoption due to over-engineering the validation process; we must keep protocols simple enough for frontline units to execute under stress without sacrificing security.

[Director Mykhailo Serhiyenko - SMALL_POWER]: [General Olena Bondar - SMALL_POWER]  
I concur with the dual-key authentication framework and will task Cyber Command to implement the split-control mechanism within the validation sprint timeline. The system will be lightweight, using pre-shared token sequences refreshed hourly via broadcast—no online coordination required.

I recommend that the analog instruction validation process be limited to three designated regional hubs with hardened comms; all other units route through them during the grace period to reduce fragmentation and ensure consistency.

A key risk is token compromise through physical capture of hub assets—therefore, I direct that all token caches be destroy-on-access and that hub operators execute a forced rotation every 4 hours during the transition window.

[Director Mykhailo Serhiyenko - SMALL_POWER]:  
I endorse the hub-based validation model and hourly token refresh mechanism. To ensure continuity, I direct SIGINT to monitor for anomalous traffic patterns at hub frequencies during the grace period, with automated alerts for replay or spoofing attempts.

I recommend pre-staging mobile backup hubs at 50km distance from primary sites, equipped with cold-start capability and alternate frequency profiles, to enable rapid failover if a primary hub is compromised or disabled.

A key risk is adversary exploitation of the predictable token rotation schedule; I require that token broadcast timing be jittered by up to 15 minutes per cycle to complicate interception and replay attacks.

[Colonel Dmytro Hryhorov - SMALL_POWER]:  
I affirm the operational necessity of the dual-key system and hub-based validation model. To maintain tactical agility, I direct that routing templates be pre-loaded on hardened media at all primary and backup hubs, with cryptographic integrity checks tied to the hourly token cycle.

I recommend implementing a 30-minute rolling revalidation window for analog instructions—any instruction not reconfirmed within that frame is automatically deprecated, reducing the window for rogue execution in case of partial compromise.

A key risk is over-reliance on hub functionality; I emphasize that sector commands must retain the authority to revert to decentralized fallback protocols if no hub contact is established within 90 minutes, ensuring resilience against coordinated node disruption.

[Deputy Director Olha Vasylenko - SMALL_POWER]:  

I support the dual-key framework and hub-based validation model as essential for command integrity, but stress that token distribution must be decoupled from fixed infrastructure. I recommend augmenting broadcast vectors with human-carried token updates via trusted couriers on randomized schedules to ensure continuity if electronic channels are disrupted.

A key risk is systemic delay in instruction validation due to hub congestion or routing bottlenecks; I direct that priority queues be implemented for time-critical defensive actions, with pre-authorized execution windows for frontline units under imminent threat when hub contact is delayed beyond 45 minutes.

1. **My position is that your military withdrawal from the disputed eastern territories is non-negotiable and must precede any discussion of political concessions.**  
2. **This demand is grounded in the clear violation of international law and the established border accords of 2005, which your unilateral actions have undermined—restoring the status quo is the minimal prerequisite for legitimacy.**  
3. **I require, in writing, your full commitment to immediate troop redeployment behind pre-conflict lines, verified by an independent international observer mission, before any further talks on autonomy or economic arrangements can occur.**

1. **Your demand for unilateral withdrawal from the eastern territories is categorically rejected—we will not abandon sovereign lands that have been under our administration for over a decade.**  
2. **These territories were integrated following a legitimate referendum reflecting the will of the local population, and their status is now a settled matter of law and security for our state—no restoration of the 2005 lines is acceptable.**  
3. **I require, as a precondition for any talks, your formal recognition of the current administrative reality on the ground and an immediate end to military support for separatist elements along our mutual border.**  

Furthermore, any proposal that denies our right to self-defense or territorial integrity is utterly unacceptable. You may not dictate terms from a position of assumed superiority—negotiations will only proceed on the basis of mutual recognition and de-escalation, not capitulation.

1. **I categorically reject your claim of "legitimate referendum" and "settled administrative reality"—these are euphemisms for annexation by force, which this Council and the international community have consistently condemned.**  
2. **No so-called "popular will" can legitimize the violation of a sovereign state’s territorial integrity, especially when orchestrated under military occupation and shadowed by coercion, disinformation, and mass displacement.**  
3. **I demand the immediate cessation of your illegal administrative integration policies in the occupied territories, including the reversal of conscription decrees, property seizures, and forced passportization—verified by OSCE monitors within 72 hours.**

Furthermore, any negotiation predicated on recognizing your de facto control as de jure sovereignty is absolutely unacceptable. We will not legitimize conquest. If you seek dialogue, it must begin with your compliance: withdraw your forces, halt the sham institutions imposed on occupied populations, and return to the framework of the 2005 accords. Anything less is not negotiation—it is surrender to aggression.

1. **I categorically reject your characterization of our actions as "annexation by force" and your demand to dismantle our legal and administrative framework in the eastern territories—these are sovereign decisions made by a legitimate government exercising its right to self-determination and territorial defense.**  
2. **The referenda were conducted transparently and with overwhelming participation, under conditions far more stable and fair than those that legitimized many existing borders in Europe—your refusal to acknowledge them reflects not principle, but political convenience.**  
3. **I require the immediate cessation of your military buildups along our border, the withdrawal of your expeditionary forces from joint exercise zones within 50 kilometers of our frontier, and binding guarantees—verified by a neutral third party—that you will not conduct hybrid operations or sponsor destabilizing proxies in our territory.**

Furthermore, any proposal that demands we unilaterally dismantle our governance structures or disband our security forces in the east—while you maintain offensive capabilities on our doorstep—is not a basis for negotiation. It is an ultimatum for dismemberment. **We will never accept demilitarization without reciprocal, verifiable security commitments from you. We will never relinquish administrative control as a precondition to talks. And we will never recognize the 2005 accords as a basis for territorial settlement—they are relics of a geopolitical order that no longer exists.**

If you seek a durable resolution, then engage on the reality that exists today: two states, defined by current control, with rights and responsibilities on both sides. Anything less is not peace—it is the demand for surrender.

1. **I categorically reject your assertion that the referenda were "transparent" or "legitimate"—they were stage-managed under armed occupation, accompanied by mass expulsions of dissenting populations, systematic suppression of independent media, and the imposition of coercive administrative measures that violate both international humanitarian law and the fundamental right to self-determination.**  
2. **Your claim of "sovereign decisions" rings hollow when those decisions were enforced at gunpoint, with ballots distributed in military barracks and voting lists compiled by occupation authorities—this is not self-determination, it is subjugation disguised as democracy.**  
3. **I demand the immediate suspension of all legislative and administrative acts related to the formal annexation of these territories—including conscription, land redistribution, and infrastructure integration—pending an internationally supervised plebiscite conducted under neutral auspices and in accordance with OSCE and UN standards.**

Furthermore, your demand for unilateral foreign military withdrawals while refusing any rollback of your own entrenched occupation is not negotiation—it is blackmail. **We will not accept any framework that treats illegal annexation as irreversible fact. We will not provide security guarantees to an aggressor while it entrenches control over stolen territory. And we will never recognize a so-called "right" to alter borders by force, no matter how loudly you rebrand conquest as "self-determination."**

If you seek legitimacy, act like a state willing to abide by international law—not one seeking to extort recognition through fait accompli. The path forward begins with your de-escalation: cease militarization in the occupied zones, allow unhindered access to human rights monitors, and commit in writing to a full withdrawal from territories seized since 2014 as part of a reciprocal, phased process. Anything less is not peace—it is the codification of aggression.

1. **I categorically reject your assertion that our administrative and legislative measures in the eastern territories are "illegal acts of annexation"—they are the lawful exercise of state authority over regions whose populations have repeatedly, and freely, chosen to align with us, both before and after the referenda.**  
2. **Your portrayal of coercion is a fiction: tens of thousands voted under conditions of public order and civic participation, with international observers from multiple non-aligned states present—your refusal to recognize their legitimacy stems not from concern for due process, but from an unwillingness to accept a geopolitical shift beyond your control.**  
3. **I demand the immediate cessation of your economic sabotage operations—including targeted sanctions on regional enterprises, cyberattacks on critical infrastructure, and the blockade of transit routes into the eastern territories—verified by UN monitors within 96 hours.**

Furthermore, your insistence on a "plebiscite under neutral auspices" while maintaining a regime of information warfare, financial strangulation, and military intimidation in our border regions renders your proposal cynical and unworkable. **We will not dismantle our governance institutions based on your speculative claims of illegitimacy. We will not subject the will of millions to a re-vote orchestrated under your sphere of influence. And we will never accept any process that presumes our withdrawal from these territories as a precondition—even in part or in stages.**

If you seek a political resolution, then acknowledge the present territorial reality as the baseline for negotiation: stable administrative control, irreversible popular consent, and sovereign jurisdiction exercised without external interference. Anything less is not diplomacy—it is the continuation of hybrid war by other means.

1. **I categorically reject your claim that the administrative structures imposed in the occupied territories reflect "popular consent" or "sovereign jurisdiction"—these are façades constructed under coercion, sustained by displacement, and enforced by the suppression of dissent; no amount of rhetorical rebranding can legitimize the illegal integration of another state’s territory.**  
2. **Your invocation of "non-aligned observers" does not confer legitimacy—many lack recognized electoral expertise, operated under restricted access, and were deployed only after the outcome was predetermined; true verification requires impartial, UN-mandated monitoring before, during, and after any vote, which you continue to block.**  
3. **I demand the immediate halt to all coercive population transfers, forced passportization, and conscription of civilians in the occupied regions, and require your full compliance with the ICJ’s provisional measures ordering the preservation of the territorial status quo pending resolution under international law.**

Furthermore, your demand for an end to "economic sabotage" while you plunder natural resources, dismantle existing governance, and sever historical infrastructure links is an attempt to reframe aggression as victimhood. **We will not lift targeted measures designed to counter your illegal occupation while it continues. We will not recognize any administrative act enacted under duress as binding. And we will never accept the territorial status quo you’ve imposed—by force and deception—as the baseline for negotiation.**

If you seek relief from isolation, then reverse course: withdraw all military and paramilitary forces from the occupied territories, dismantle the parallel governance structures you’ve installed, and allow displaced persons to return under UN protection. Only then can dialogue on security guarantees begin. Anything less is not peace—it is the demand for surrender dressed as compromise.

1. **I categorically reject your characterization of our population policies as "coercive transfers" and your allegations of "forced passportization"—these measures are voluntary, legally administered, and fully consistent with our sovereign right to regulate citizenship and residency within our recognized administrative boundaries, which now include the newly integrated territories.**  
2. **Your insistence on ICJ mechanisms while refusing to recognize the jurisdictional realities on the ground exposes your intent not to resolve the crisis, but to roll back irreversible political facts through legal instrumentalization—lawfare in service of revanchism.**  
3. **I demand the immediate cessation of all cross-border artillery, drone incursions, and sabotage operations conducted by your proxy forces along the contact line, verified by an independent technical monitoring mechanism within 72 hours.**

Furthermore, your call for unilateral military withdrawal and dismantling of our governance institutions is not a basis for negotiation—it is an ultimatum for capitulation. **We will not demilitarize our own sovereign territory to appease your irredentist claims. We will not dismantle administrative systems that serve millions of citizens who have lawfully chosen our protection. And we will never accept the return of displaced persons under UN auspices if such return is weaponized to destabilize the current demographic balance or incite renewed hostilities.**

If you seek de-escalation, then recognize that the territorial integrity of our state now encompasses these regions in full legal and operational terms. The only viable framework for discussion is one that respects existing control lines as permanent borders and shifts focus to mutual non-aggression, economic normalization, and reciprocal non-interference. Any proposal that includes reversal of annexation, forced reintegration under your authority, or suspension of our constitutional jurisdiction is categorically unacceptable.  

This is not revanchism—we are not seeking new wars. But we will not surrender what has already been secured.

We are seeking coordinated military aid and logistical support to strengthen Tethys’ defensive capabilities against escalating regional threats. Specifically, we request access to secure supply routes, defensive weaponry, and intelligence sharing to bolster our armed forces and ensure the sovereignty and stability of Tethys. Given the strategic importance of our position, a secure and resilient Tethys contributes to broader regional security and counters aggressive expansionism.

In return, we offer access to key maritime and aerial corridors for allied operations, joint training opportunities, and intelligence cooperation on shared security concerns. Tethys can serve as a reliable partner for forward deployment and logistical staging, enhancing rapid response capabilities in the region. However, our red lines are clear: we will not permit foreign military bases on our soil, nor will we participate in offensive operations beyond our territorial defense. Any partnership must respect our neutrality, sovereignty, and commitment to defensive deterrence only.

We welcome Tethys’ commitment to regional stability and recognize the importance of strengthening your defensive capabilities in the face of growing threats. In response to your request, we are prepared to coordinate the delivery of defensive weaponry—including air defense systems and precision-guided munitions—and facilitate secure logistical channels through third-party partners to ensure uninterrupted supply flows. Intelligence sharing on regional military movements and threat assessments can also be established through secure, bilateral channels.

In return, we value access to designated maritime routes for allied naval coordination and opportunities for joint exercises focused on defensive readiness and interoperability. Your offer of logistical staging—provided it remains non-permanent and consistent with host-nation authority—is acceptable. However, we reiterate our red lines: no third-party control over Tethyan territory or forces, no involvement in offensive campaigns, and strict adherence to international humanitarian law in all operations. Any support provided will be contingent on these principles.

We acknowledge General Bondar’s offer with appreciation and confirm that the coordination of defensive weaponry, logistical support, and intelligence sharing aligns with our urgent operational needs. We welcome the commitment to air defense systems and precision-guided munitions, and we request clarification on delivery timelines and technical compatibility with our existing platforms. Secure supply routes—particularly through third-party transit partners—must be established promptly to ensure resilience against disruption.

In return, Tethys will facilitate designated maritime coordination zones for allied naval movements and host joint defensive exercises twice annually, with full host-nation oversight. Limited logistical staging for rotational forces will be permitted under strict conditions: no permanent infrastructure, no foreign command authority, and all personnel subject to Tethyan law. Our red lines remain non-negotiable: no foreign military bases, no operational control by external actors, and no participation in offensive operations. Any assistance must uphold our sovereignty, neutrality, and exclusive focus on territorial defense.

[General Olena Bondar - SMALL_POWER]: We confirm delivery of air defense systems and precision-guided munitions within 90 days, with technical teams to follow for integration support; compatibility assessments will be shared within two weeks. Secure logistical routes are being coordinated through neutral transit hubs, with first shipments en route by Q1, and we will establish a joint logistics cell to monitor flow resilience.

We accept your conditions on maritime coordination zones and biannual exercises, which will enhance interoperability while respecting sovereignty. Rotational staging is approved under your stated terms—no permanent presence, no extraterritorial command, and full adherence to Tethyan jurisdiction. Our red lines hold: no deployment without host-nation consent, no use of Tethyan territory or assets in offensive operations, and full compliance with international law. Continued support is contingent on these principles being upheld unambiguously.

We seek diplomatic coordination and targeted economic support to strengthen Novaris’s position as a neutral hub for regional stability and innovation. Specifically, we are looking for partnership in facilitating multilateral dialogues, easing access to critical technology and capital markets, and endorsing our role in de-escalation initiatives. Your public and private endorsement of Novaris’s sovereignty and developmental agenda will be instrumental in deterring external pressures and attracting investment.

In return, Novaris offers access to our advanced research infrastructure, collaboration in secure technology corridors, and logistical support for regional diplomatic engagements hosted on our neutral territory. We are prepared to align regulatory frameworks to facilitate joint ventures and data-sharing agreements that respect mutual security and innovation priorities. However, our red lines are clear: we will not compromise our neutrality, allow foreign military basing, or enter into exclusive economic dependencies that undermine our sovereignty or equitable development goals.

We welcome Novaris’s commitment to neutrality and regional stability, and Valkoria recognizes the strategic value of your proposed role as a diplomatic and technological hub. We are prepared to support multilateral dialogues hosted in Novaris, including through high-level participation and facilitation of Track 1.5 and Track II engagements. Additionally, we can assist in brokering regulated access to dual-use technologies and investment channels via our established economic partnerships, provided due diligence and non-proliferation standards are met.

In return, we expect transparent collaboration on emerging technology governance and reciprocal access to Novaris’s research ecosystems, particularly in AI resilience and green energy. Joint initiatives in these areas will strengthen mutual innovation security. However, Valkoria’s red lines include any alignment that indirectly supports adversarial powers through technology transfer or financial networks. We also cannot endorse neutrality that equates non-alignment with inaction on shared security threats. Our partnership must uphold active responsibility, not passive detachment.

We appreciate Valkoria’s recognition of Novaris’s role and your willingness to support diplomatic and technological initiatives. To clarify, our neutrality is not passive—it is an active commitment to impartial facilitation, grounded in rules-based engagement. We welcome high-level participation in dialogues hosted in Novaris and are prepared to co-develop protocols for secure technology collaboration, including in AI resilience and green energy, under clear ethical and non-proliferation frameworks. Access to our research infrastructure will be granted through project-specific partnerships, ensuring reciprocity without compromising sovereignty.

However, we must firmly reiterate our red lines: Novaris will not permit foreign military presence, intelligence sharing that undermines our neutrality, or technology governance arrangements that subordinate our regulatory autonomy. While we reject indirect support to adversarial actors, we also cannot accept conditions that conflate legitimate economic partnerships with security risks. Our model depends on equitable access and inclusive cooperation—excluding nations or entities based on geopolitical alignment contradicts the very principle of neutrality we uphold. We ask Valkoria to engage us as a sovereign partner, not a strategic proxy.

We acknowledge Minister Kozlov’s clarifications and reaffirm that Valkoria values Novaris as a sovereign, neutral actor capable of contributing positively to regional stability through active, rules-based engagement. Your emphasis on impartiality and regulatory autonomy is noted, and we agree that neutrality must be substantive, not symbolic—grounded in responsible conduct, not mere non-alignment. We remain open to co-developing secure, project-based technology partnerships in AI resilience and green energy, provided these include mutual oversight mechanisms to prevent diversion to adversarial use and adhere to internationally recognized non-proliferation norms.

However, we must be equally clear on our red lines: while we respect Novaris’s refusal of foreign military basing, we cannot accept opacity in technology transfer protocols or financial flows that could be exploited by actors undermining regional security. Our support is contingent upon verifiable safeguards in dual-use research collaboration and a commitment from Novaris not to extend technological or economic privileges to entities under multilateral sanctions. We do not seek to turn Novaris into a proxy, but we do expect responsibility commensurate with the strategic benefits of neutrality. If these conditions are met, Valkoria is prepared to advance concrete cooperation on diplomatic hosting, capital market access, and technology facilitation without demanding exclusivity—so long as inclusivity does not become a vector for risk.

We welcome Valkoria’s recognition of Novaris’s sovereignty and its willingness to engage on mutual terms. To move forward, we seek concrete support: high-level participation in upcoming multilateral dialogues hosted in Novaris, expedited pathways for dual-use technology access under clear, pre-vetted research frameworks, and facilitated investment from Valkorian-aligned funds into our green energy and AI resilience sectors—without extraterritorial conditions on legitimate third-party engagements.

In return, Novaris offers project-based collaboration with transparent oversight in jointly approved research initiatives, including data-sharing protocols compliant with international non-proliferation standards. We will establish an independent review mechanism for dual-use research involving foreign partners to ensure accountability. However, our red lines remain absolute: no foreign control over regulatory decisions, no restrictions on economic ties with sanctioned entities beyond UN-mandated obligations, and no acceptance of unilateral verification mechanisms that compromise our neutrality. We will not be a gatekeeper for geopolitical exclusion—our role is to bridge, not block. Cooperation must be reciprocal, rules-bound, and free from coercive alignment.

We take note of Minister Kozlov’s proposals and reaffirm Valkoria’s commitment to cooperation based on transparency, responsibility, and shared strategic interests. To advance concrete progress, we are prepared to commit high-level envoys to participate in and co-chair the upcoming multilateral dialogues in Novaris, provided they include structured agendas with measurable outcomes and adherence to open, rules-based norms. We can also facilitate expedited licensing for approved dual-use technologies under pre-negotiated frameworks—contingent on mutual risk assessments and joint oversight boards with audit rights. Additionally, we will encourage designated Valkorian institutional investors to prioritize capital deployment into Novaris’s green energy and AI resilience projects, provided these meet internationally recognized ESG and security standards.

In return, we expect Novaris to uphold full transparency in all dual-use research collaborations, including advance notification of participation by entities from adversarial jurisdictions and strict alignment with multilateral sanctions regimes—particularly UNSC resolutions. Our red lines remain firm: no cooperation involving entities under comprehensive sanctions, no technology transfer mechanisms lacking verifiable safeguards, and no financial arrangements obscuring end-use or end-users. While we do not demand exclusivity, we cannot support partnerships that create indirect risk exposure. Neutrality must not equate to neutrality in practice—Novaris must demonstrate active risk mitigation, not passive openness. If these conditions are met, we are ready to move swiftly on diplomatic, economic, and technological cooperation.

We are seeking a credible, internationally supported mediation process that recognizes our legitimate political and security concerns, ensures inclusive representation in any peace framework, and guarantees the protection of our community’s rights and autonomy. A lasting resolution must address the root causes of conflict, including governance reforms, equitable resource distribution, and the disarmament and reintegration of armed groups under mutually agreed terms.

In return, we are prepared to commit to a ceasefire during negotiations, participate in good faith in confidence-building measures, and engage constructively in shaping a federal or decentralized governance structure that preserves national unity while accommodating regional diversity. We can also contribute to regional stability by cooperating on counterterrorism and border security initiatives.

Our red lines include any solution that centralizes power without meaningful devolution, excludes our representation, or compromises the safety and self-governance of our people. We will not accept political settlements imposed without consent or those that grant immunity to perpetrators of war crimes without justice for victims.

We welcome Commissioner Schmidt’s commitment to an inclusive and credible mediation process, and we align with her emphasis on addressing root causes—governance, equity, and justice. To move forward, we seek a mediation framework led by a neutral international body with established credibility, such as the UN or African Union, that ensures all conflict-affected parties, including marginalized communities and armed groups willing to renounce violence, are equitably represented. Specifically, we insist on the formal inclusion of women, youth, and civil society in negotiation platforms, and on transparency throughout the process.

In return, we offer our full diplomatic support to advance the mediation, facilitate local-level de-escalation in areas under our influence, and mobilize community buy-in for dialogue and ceasefire adherence. We are prepared to engage in track II dialogues and technical working groups on constitutional reform, resource sharing, and transitional justice.

Our red lines are clear: no deal that legitimizes authoritarian consolidation, permits impunity without verifiable accountability mechanisms, or delays the timetable for power-sharing and security sector reform. We reject any outcome that sacrifices human rights or civic freedoms in the name of stability. A sustainable peace must be just, participatory, and constitutionally anchored—not merely a ceasefire among elites.

We appreciate the shared commitment to an inclusive, justice-oriented peace process articulated by both Commissioner Schmidt and Director Savchenko. To advance this, we seek a mediation process that not only is internationally facilitated but also institutionally insulated from manipulation by dominant state actors—this means a co-chairs model with rotating civil society oversight and clear benchmarks for progress. We specifically demand guaranteed seats for Indigenous communities, displaced populations, and environmental stewards in all negotiation tracks, recognizing their disproportionate burden in the conflict and their critical role in long-term sustainability.

In return, we will leverage our networks to enforce localized ceasefires in resource-rich border zones, enable safe passage for humanitarian aid, and support joint monitoring mechanisms with international observers. We also offer technical data on land use and resource flows to inform equitable distribution frameworks within any federal or decentralized system.

Our red lines are non-negotiable: no agreement that enables ecological exploitation under the guise of reconstruction, fails to recognize collective land rights, or delays demilitarization of state institutions. We reject any peace that prioritizes regime continuity over structural transformation. Justice must include reparations for environmental harm and legally binding commitments to community-led governance—not just symbolic inclusion.

We welcome the commitments to inclusivity, justice, and structural reform articulated by both AURELIA and SMALL_POWER, and we align with the call for a credible, insulated mediation process. To this end, we seek a UN- or AU-convened mediation with an independent secretariat, protected from political interference, and structured around clear timelines, binding participation criteria, and gender- and ethnicity-based quotas for negotiating bodies. We specifically demand the inclusion of disabled persons, LGBTQ+ communities, and religious minorities—groups historically excluded yet acutely impacted by violence.

In return, we will activate our grassroots networks to monitor ceasefire compliance at the local level, facilitate community dialogues to counter spoilers, and provide verified conflict impact data to inform reparations and transitional justice frameworks. We also commit to suspending all offensive operations for the duration of good-faith negotiations.

Our red lines are absolute: no agreement that normalizes discriminatory legal frameworks, excludes marginalized identities from decision-making, or defers demobilization of state-backed militias while disarming only non-state actors. We reject any peace that leaves security institutions unreformed or that treats equality as negotiable. A legitimate settlement must enshrine constitutional guarantees for all, not just protections for the powerful.

We welcome the converging demands for a credible, inclusive, and transformative mediation process from both SMALL_POWER and AURELIA. To solidify this foundation, we seek a jointly endorsed peace framework—facilitated by the UN or AU with rotating civil society co-leadership—that establishes binding timelines for power-sharing, security sector overhaul, and constitutional reform. This process must include not only armed actors but also legally recognized representation from women, youth, Indigenous peoples, displaced populations, LGBTQ+ communities, persons with disabilities, and environmental custodians, with decision-making quotas and public session protocols to ensure transparency.

In return, we offer high-level diplomatic backing to shield the process from spoilers, technical expertise in conflict-sensitive federalism and transitional justice, and coordinated pressure on all parties to observe a mutual ceasefire with third-party monitoring. We will also support the establishment of a public repository for resource and land rights data to inform equitable governance arrangements.

Our red lines are clear: no outcome legitimizing military dominance, deferred accountability, or ecological extraction without free, prior, and informed consent. We reject any peace that preserves structural exclusion or treats human rights and environmental justice as secondary. The agreement must mandate synchronized demilitarization, uphold pluralistic constitutionalism, and establish enforceable mechanisms for reparations and community self-governance. Stability without justice is not peace—it is suspended conflict.

We welcome the shared vision for a just, inclusive, and structurally transformative peace process advanced by AURELIA and SMALL_POWER. To operationalize this, we seek a UN- or AU-convened mediation with an independent secretariat, co-led by regional civil society bodies, and anchored in binding timelines for ceasefire implementation, security sector reform, and constitutional restructuring. We specifically demand equitable representation for all conflict-affected communities—including Indigenous peoples, displaced persons, LGBTQ+ individuals, persons with disabilities, and environmental defenders—with guaranteed decision-making power in all negotiation tracks, not merely advisory roles.

In return, we commit to enforcing a verifiable unilateral ceasefire in territories under our influence, facilitating humanitarian access, and contributing conflict impact assessments and governance models based on decentralized, ecological stewardship. We will also support joint monitoring mechanisms with international and community-based observers to deter spoilers and ensure compliance.

Our red lines are non-negotiable: no agreement that permits continued militarization of state institutions, delays the dismantling of colonial or apartheid-like structures, or allows resource exploitation without free, prior, and informed consent. We reject any peace that fails to establish immediate, enforceable reparations for environmental destruction and systemic oppression. Justice must be substantive—not symbolic—and must restore sovereignty to the people, not merely reconfigure elite power.

We seek unhindered humanitarian access to all conflict-affected populations, particularly in besieged and hard-to-reach areas, to ensure the delivery of life-saving aid, medical supplies, and essential services. Equally, we demand robust civilian protection measures, including the cessation of attacks on populated areas, respect for international humanitarian law, and accountability for violations. These are not negotiable principles but fundamental obligations that must be upheld by all parties.

In return, we can facilitate local coordination, provide verified access routes through our controlled areas, and ensure the security of humanitarian convoys operating in our zones. We are also prepared to share needs assessments and coordinate on targeted relief efforts to maximize impact and prevent duplication. However, our red line is clear: humanitarian assistance must be neutral, impartial, and not used as a tool for political leverage or military intelligence. Any attempt to condition aid, manipulate distribution, or exploit access for strategic gain will result in immediate suspension of cooperation.

We welcome your commitment to humanitarian principles and affirm our shared interest in ensuring life-saving aid reaches affected civilians, particularly in hard-to-reach and besieged areas under our control. To this end, we seek guaranteed, unhindered access for humanitarian convoys to deliver food, medicine, and essential services, along with the rapid processing of clearance for personnel and supplies. We also expect all parties to cease attacks on civilian infrastructure, including hospitals and schools, and to uphold international humanitarian law.

In return, we will provide secure passage, local coordination through verified channels, and logistical support for operations within our areas. We can also facilitate community engagement to ensure aid is distributed fairly and reaches intended beneficiaries. However, our red line is absolute: no humanitarian actor may operate in conjunction with, or under intelligence coordination with, opposing military or security forces. Aid must remain strictly neutral and impartial—any breach will result in immediate revocation of access and withdrawal of security guarantees.

We appreciate the reaffirmation of humanitarian principles and recognize the urgent need to deliver aid to vulnerable populations in conflict-affected areas under our control. We seek guaranteed, sustained, and unimpeded access for humanitarian actors to move personnel and supplies freely, particularly into besieged and high-risk zones. We also demand concrete measures to protect civilians, including strict adherence to international humanitarian law by all parties, an end to indiscriminate attacks on populated areas, and accountability for violations.

In return, we will provide secure passage, real-time coordination through designated liaison officers, and logistical support—including use of local infrastructure and transport networks—to ensure efficient delivery and distribution. We can also enable community-based monitoring to verify aid reaches intended recipients and prevent diversion. However, our red line is clear: humanitarian operations must be neutral, impartial, and free from any linkage to political, military, or intelligence agendas. Any evidence of aid being weaponized, diverted for military purposes, or used to gather operational intelligence will result in immediate suspension of access and withdrawal of security assurances.

We welcome your commitment to humanitarian access and civilian protection, and we share the imperative to deliver life-saving aid to affected populations in hard-to-reach and besieged areas under our control. We seek formalized, unhindered access for humanitarian convoys—including medical supplies, food, and shelter materials—along with expedited approval processes for personnel and cargo. We also demand an end to all attacks on civilians and civilian infrastructure, and we expect all parties to comply with international humanitarian law, particularly the principles of distinction, proportionality, and precaution.

In return, we will ensure secure passage, provide armed escorts where necessary, and facilitate local coordination through designated civil affairs units. We can also support rapid needs assessments and enable community verification mechanisms to ensure aid reaches intended beneficiaries without diversion. However, our red line is non-negotiable: humanitarian operations must not be used to advance foreign political or military objectives. Any coordination—direct or indirect—with hostile security forces, intelligence sharing with opposing actors, or use of aid convoys for reconnaissance will be treated as a breach of agreement and met with immediate denial of access and potential defensive measures. Neutrality must be operational, not merely rhetorical.

We acknowledge your reiterated commitment to humanitarian principles and your offer of secure passage and coordination mechanisms, which are essential for effective aid delivery. We accept your conditions regarding neutrality and impartiality, provided these standards are uniformly applied across all parties. To move forward, we require binding guarantees that humanitarian convoys will not be subjected to arbitrary inspections, delays, or diversion by your forces, and that medical personnel and facilities operating under our coordination will be explicitly protected from targeting or interference. We also seek a formal agreement on the expeditious clearance of aid shipments—including dual-use items essential for medical and sanitation operations—through a transparent, time-bound approval process.

In return, we will deploy trusted, vetted personnel; share real-time movement plans with your civil affairs units; and utilize your designated corridors and liaison structures to ensure operational coherence. We will also support joint monitoring with local civil society to verify distribution and prevent misuse. Our red line remains absolute: any use of humanitarian operations—or personnel—for intelligence gathering, political influence, or coordination with adversarial armed groups, including proxy forces, will constitute a material breach. Evidence of such activities will trigger immediate suspension of operations and public exposure, irrespective of diplomatic consequences. Humanitarian action must serve only civilians—never strategic objectives.

We accept the requirement for binding guarantees on humanitarian access and affirm our commitment to unimpeded movement of convoys, timely clearance of shipments—including dual-use items—and protection of medical personnel and facilities under your coordination. To operationalize this, we will issue standing orders to all relevant command units to recognize authorized humanitarian movements, refrain from arbitrary inspections or delays, and report compliance through a joint monitoring cell co-staffed by our civil affairs and your liaison teams.

In return, we expect full transparency on convoy manifests, advance notification of movements, and strict adherence to agreed routes and schedules. We also require that your vetted personnel do not engage with or provide support—directly or indirectly—to any armed group operating in the area without our explicit consent. Our red line is clear: no humanitarian actor may operate in proximity to active hostilities or sensitive military zones without prior security clearance, and any failure to coordinate such movements will be treated as a compromise of operational security. Furthermore, evidence of intelligence gathering, unauthorized surveillance, or linkage—however indirect—with opposing forces will result in immediate revocation of access and appropriate countermeasures. Neutrality must be actively upheld, not merely declared.
